body:
9.40 THESIS -- PART 2 of 3 (Section 3: methodology audit synthesis).

Note: part 1/3 is committed locally but hasn't reached GitHub yet -- this machine is low on free memory right now (1.65GB free of 8GB) and git's push needs a ~500MB buffer it can't get. Not a repo problem (repo is 868KB). Retrying automatically on future ticks; nothing lost, just delayed.

=== SECTION 3: WHAT CLASS OF ERROR DO THE 9.38/9.39 FINDINGS REPRESENT ===

Listing them side by side: the Bayesian belief that reset to uniform every call instead of persisting: LOOKS like a probabilistic model, ACTS like a stateless heuristic wearing Bayesian vocabulary. The ComponentWeights field collision (reversal/directional/predictive silently merged into volatility/momentum/pattern): a rename that changed the STORAGE without updating the LOGIC that assumed the old shape still existed. The SaveSystemState/SealArmedSave version mismatch: two code paths that were clearly edited at different times by different reasoning, never reconciled against each other. The dead SL-adjustment code in DetermineOptimalTPSL: a fix that computes a real value and then gets silently overwritten by code written without awareness the fix existed. The fabricated R-multiples: a fallback path that prioritised 'never block the pipeline' over 'never report a number I don't have'. The regime-count inconsistency (9 vs 12): documentation and one array's actual size drifted apart from the classifier's real behavior.

These are not six random bugs. They share ONE pattern: every single one is a case where two parts of the system that USED to agree (or were assumed to agree) stopped agreeing, and nothing detected the drift because nothing ever re-validates that agreement. The system has plenty of mechanisms that compute a NEW value carefully (the Kelly sizing math, the Phase 2 IC computation, the regime hysteresis logic are all genuinely sound). What it doesn't have is any mechanism that checks whether a PREVIOUSLY-established invariant (this struct has these fields; this save format has these fields in this order; this array holds N valid indices; this adjustment I compute actually gets used downstream) still holds after later edits touch adjacent code.

This is a structural blindspot, not a competence problem -- the actual math throughout (Kelly, IC computation, hysteresis, hard risk gates) is sound where I checked it carefully. The pattern is specifically: extensive iterative live-log-driven patching (visible everywhere as 'CRITICAL FIX', 'User's logs show X', SLS12/SLS13/SLS19 tagged revisions) without a mechanism to verify that a new patch doesn't silently break an assumption an OLDER patch relied on. The version-file evidence (9191 vs 9196, calibTs added to one save path and not the other) is the clearest single artifact of this: two people/sessions/passes each made a locally-reasonable change to 'the calibration save format' without checking the other one.

WHAT THIS REVEALS ABOUT ACTUAL BLINDSPOTS: the system is good at LOCAL optimization (tune this weight, add this gate, fix this specific reported symptom) and weak at INVARIANT PRESERVATION across that iterative process. 9.40 shouldn't just be another round of local fixes -- the thesis in Part 3 is that the highest-leverage change is making a small number of currently-implicit invariants explicit and hard to silently violate (a single regime-strategy accessor instead of five raw array indexes is a small example of this already done in 9.39; the SAME principle applied to the file-format version number, to the growth-rate/edge-projection logic that I found exists in at least two different places with different formulas -- CalculateDefensibleDailyGrowthRate() that I added and the 'GAME PLAN' projection already in RunMarketCalibration() -- is the pattern to generalize).

ADDITIONAL FINDING surfaced while researching this section: Phase 5/6 ghost (simulated) trades are recorded with a FLAT, FIXED R-multiple (2.5 on a simulated TP hit, -1.5 on a simulated SL hit -- see RecordGhostTrade()) regardless of what TP/SL distance was actually used for that specific simulated trade. Phase 6 is the gate that decides whether the system is allowed to go live at all, based partly on profit factor computed from these ghost trades. If the EA's real TP/SL logic (regime/session/volatility-aware, per DetermineOptimalTPSL) produces a genuinely variable RR per trade, but the validation gate scores every simulated trade as if it were always exactly 2.5R or -1.5R, the go-live decision is being made on a systematically simplified version of the system's own actual behavior. This is exactly the same class of bug as the fabricated live R-multiples I fixed in 9.39 -- same root cause (a shortcut that trades accuracy for simplicity in exactly the place accuracy matters most), just in the calibration path instead of the live path.

(Part 3/3: the exponential-improvement case and the prioritised implementation plan.)
